original_abstract:
In the Batak Toba community, rigid traditional customs often relegate women to the authority of male relatives and husbands, frequently confining them to the role of *parhobas* (servants) during family and ceremonial events. This research explores the transformative potential of Ivone Gebara’s theological thought in addressing these entrenched gender inequalities. Rooted in the principles of Liberation Theology, Gebara’s work emphasizes the necessity of connecting Christian faith with the lived experiences of those marginalized by socio-economic and patriarchal injustices. Through a comprehensive literature review, this study examines Gebara’s critique of patriarchal structures within both the Church and society, advocating for an inclusive, egalitarian interpretation of women's experiences to foster social change.

The paper contextualizes Gebara’s ideas—specifically her focus on ecofeminism and the "maternal" care of the Divine—within the Indonesian Batak Toba socio-cultural framework. By drawing parallels between Gebara’s thought and local cosmological symbols like *Si Boru Deang Parujar*, the research highlights an indigenous foundation for gender equality. Ultimately, Gebara’s insights provide a roadmap for enriching public narratives, supporting feminist education, and encouraging open dialogue regarding religious norms and women’s roles. The study concludes that applying these theological reflections can support a movement toward a more just, inclusive, and sustainable social transformation within the Batak Toba community.
humanized_abstract:
Customs and traditions in Batak Toba society usually make the woman submitted to her father, her brother and also to her husband and obliges her to be confined in a position as a parhobas or servant in the family and also in society, when participating in the organisation of the family and ceremonial affairs. This research is aiming to explore further the impact or effects of Ivone Gebara’s theological theory on the customary rights in relation to solving the customary problem faced by women in Batak Toba community. Ivone Gebara in her theories of theological thinks tries to link the Christian faith with the lives of the marginalised in society, be it socioeconomically or be it due to the structures of patriarchy within the Church and the society and so trying to offer an alternative feminist reading of the women’s experiences which is supposed to act as the tool of change in the society. This research was based on the review of some literature produced by experts of Ivone Gebara’s works.

Paper References Title: Gebara's Theology in Light of the Batak Toba Social Reality This study attempt to relate and verify Gebara’s thoughts which are very relevant to Indonesian reality; namely ecofeminism and the ‘maternal’ aspect of the Divine in relation to the culture of Batak Toba. It starts by describing the reality of Batak Toba women and discussing some cosmological images in Batak culture —*Si Boru Deang Parujar*— which can function as a foundation for a cultural movement that advances gender equality. Gebara’s ideas function as a scientific source that can enrich public consciousness and is therefore capable of developing feminist education for discussing religion and women in accordance with the times so that it can serve as a way of expounding the universe of values needed to realisze social change in the Batak Toba community that is just, humane and sustainable.
